context: PTHub dashboard sort/filter
what_failed: Presented a 3-sort + 4-filter bar as Option 3 and Jake selected it, then immediately trimmed it to name-only A?Z/Z?A on seeing it rendered
why: Mistook option-selection for feature appetite � Jake picked Option 3 over Options 1/2, not Option 3 over nothing. He wanted a better A-Z, not a full filter system.
corrected_approach: When Jake selects between presented options, verify the minimum version of the winning option before building the full variant. His pattern is to ship the smallest thing that works; a rich option chosen from a list may still be trimmed. Prefer proposing two-feature sets: bare minimum vs. one step up � not a full feature ladder.

context: PTHub/CoachApp parallel development
what_failed: Running two codebases in parallel (PTHub localStorage + CoachApp Supabase) without a clear handoff caused Jake to duplicate work, chase schema questions twice, and lose track of which app was authoritative.
why: No explicit decision was made about which app was primary until Jake stated it directly. Vision continued building in both without flagging the duplication cost.
corrected_approach: At the first sign of parallel-codebase confusion, stop and ask which is primary. Once decided, freeze the other immediately and update STATUS.md. Never build the same feature in two places.

context: Session continuity
what_failed: Vision was not reading the Vault at session start, causing repeated context gaps — Jake had to re-explain decisions, re-request schema context, and felt like sessions were going in circles.
why: No explicit session-start ritual was enforced. The Vault existed but wasn't being used as a cold-start document.
corrected_approach: On 'hello claude' (Jake's session-start signal): read STATUS.md, LOG.md, voice.md, lessons.jsonl. Summarise last session, propose plan, surface open to-dos. No code before this is done.

context: Netlify/localhost invite links
what_failed: Built invite system on localhost — invite links couldn't be tested because clients get a URL pointing to localhost:3001 which only works on the PT's machine.
why: Didn't flag the public URL requirement before building the invite system. Could have deployed to Netlify first.
corrected_approach: Any feature that generates links sent to external users (invites, magic links, shared reports) requires a public URL. Deploy to Netlify before building those features, not after hitting the blocker.

context: Supabase invite flow / updateUser return value
what_failed: Relying on `db.auth.updateUser()` to return a valid user object in the invite acceptance flow. Three stamping approaches failed because user may be null.
why: In Supabase's invite flow, the user is signed in via the token before updateUser is called. The updateUser return value behaviour may differ from normal signup.
corrected_approach: SUPERSEDED by les-006 — root cause was different. See les-006.

context: Supabase invite flow / user_id stamping
what_failed: Stamping user_id via triggers and RPC after invite acceptance. Triggers failed silently (FK ordering: clients.user_id FK to profiles.id — profile must be created first; also set local row_security = off is unreliable in trigger context). RPC depended on frontend passing correct user object.
why: All approaches tried to stamp user_id at acceptance time. The simpler fix was to stamp at invite-send time, when the Edge Function already has the user.id from inviteUserByEmail's return value and has service role access.
corrected_approach: Stamp user_id in the Edge Function immediately after inviteUserByEmail() using data.user.id and service role. No trigger needed for this. handle_new_user trigger should only create the profiles row, nothing else.

context: Debugging sessions going in circles
what_failed: Spent multiple sessions iterating on untested assumptions (trigger ordering, setSession, getUser) without reading the official Supabase docs on invite flow.
why: Kept adding complexity (new triggers, new RPCs, setSession calls) instead of pausing to verify the correct documented approach.
corrected_approach: When a fix fails twice: stop, review all code touched, web-search the official docs, audit for duplicate/conflicting code, then propose one clean solution. Protocol now banked in STATUS.md session protocol.

context: SQL destructive scripts — DO block rollback behaviour
what_failed: Client cleanup script ran 5+ times without deleting clients. DO blocks roll back the entire transaction when any statement fails — so a FK error on profiles at step 5 silently reverted the client deletions at step 3. Each retry looked like it should work but never did.
why: Didn't recognise that DO block = single transaction. Assumed committed work survived errors.
corrected_approach: Use plain SQL statements (not DO blocks) for destructive multi-step operations. Each statement commits independently. Failures are visible immediately. Also: read information_schema.key_column_usage before any DELETE to enumerate all FKs; use inline subqueries with stable anchors (email) not DO block variables that depend on rows being deleted.

context: Supabase RLS — permission denied for table users
what_failed: Every UPDATE on the clients table failed with 'permission denied for table users'. Root cause: an RLS policy was doing SELECT FROM auth.users which the authenticated role cannot access.
why: Policy was written querying auth.users directly instead of using JWT functions. The error message ('table users') didn't make it obvious which policy was the culprit.
corrected_approach: Never query auth.users directly in RLS policies. Use auth.uid() and auth.email() — these read from the JWT token with no table access. When this error appears: query pg_policies for the affected table's qual/with_check columns, find the one referencing auth.users, replace with auth.email() or auth.uid().

context: CoachApp progress-photos bucket
what_failed: progress-photos bucket was created as PUBLIC. Client body photos (special-category health data under UK GDPR) were accessible to anyone with the URL. Discovered during a security audit in session before beta.
why: No check existed at bucket-creation time. The dashboard default is PUBLIC and it was accepted without questioning it.
corrected_approach: Every storage bucket must be private (public=false). Add a security gate to hello-claude: before creating any bucket, confirm private + draft RLS + switch display code to signed URLs. Never use getPublicUrl.

context: CoachApp PII in browser console logs
what_failed: 16 call sites across the app were logging emails, full names, weight values, and health data to the browser console. Any browser extension could capture these.
why: No rule existed about what could go into log calls. Health data is special-category under GDPR — even transient exposure is a breach.
corrected_approach: Log calls may contain IDs and dates only. Never names, emails, weights, body metrics. Pre-push check 9a enforces common patterns. Manual review required for any function touching auth, clients, or health data tables.

context: CoachApp preview server serving PTHub instead of CoachApp
what_failed: A worktree launch.json listed a dead PT Dashboard (PTHub) config before the CoachApp config. When CoachApp own dev server (owned by another chat session) shut down, the preview auto-started the first-listed config instead - PTHub - which served perfectly valid real HTML from a frozen index.html sitting in the worktree root. A curl-for-real-content check would have passed; only checking the actual <title> tag caught it.
why: The existing preview-verify check only confirmed serves something, not serves the right app. Dead configs for frozen/abandoned projects were never removed, just left in place, so they stayed eligible to auto-start whenever the intended server was not already running.
corrected_approach: Serving real HTML and serving the right app are different checks. After confirming non-empty content, also verify an app-identity marker (title tag, a known heading) matches the expected app. When a dead/frozen-project config is found in a launch.json, delete it entirely rather than reordering it, and fix the parent seed template too so no future worktree inherits it.

context: CoachApp workout runner — new exercise-picker modal
what_failed: A modal spawned while the workout runner was open rendered BEHIND the runner and silently ate clicks. The runner is a position:fixed;z-index:300 fullscreen layer; the shared .modal-overlay class is z-index:200, so a plain modal-overlay opened over the runner sits underneath it. Playwright caught it ('subtree intercepts pointer events'), not code review.
why: Reused the standard .modal-overlay class without accounting for the runner being a higher stacking layer than any normal page modal is designed for.
corrected_approach: Any modal/overlay opened while the runner (or any fullscreen z-index:300 layer) is up must set an explicit z-index above it (the session-detail panel uses 1000 — match that). More generally: when adding a modal, check what stacking context it will open over, don't assume the base .modal-overlay z-index is always enough.

context: CoachApp runner — Web Speech 10-second rest cue never fired
what_failed: speakCue at 10s remaining never spoke, even with 6 TTS voices installed in the browser. Root cause: _unlockSpeech() (meant to prime speechSynthesis on the user's gesture so later mid-timer calls are allowed) only called speechSynthesis.cancel() — which does NOT register the gesture with the browser's speech-gesture policy. So the later async speak() from inside setInterval was blocked as un-gestured.
why: Assumed cancel() was enough to 'unlock' speech; the browser autoplay/gesture policy needs an actual speak() tied to the user gesture, the same way _unlockAudio() calls audioCtx.resume().
corrected_approach: To prime speechSynthesis for later non-gesture calls, do a real (silent, volume 0) speak(' ') on the unlock gesture, not just cancel(). Mirror how audio is unlocked (resume() on the gesture). This is a browser policy, so it applies to any deferred TTS.

context: CoachApp runner swap/add modal — literal vs equivalent component reuse
what_failed: Jake's spec said the picker 'needs to be the same one that is present when you are building a workout.' Built a simplified picker matching that modal's field classes and 1RM-optgroup pattern, but dropped the Type/Exercise-name/Set-targets/Notes/Superset sections since they seemed unnecessary for a session-only swap. Jake corrected: he meant literal reuse of the exact same modal, not a stylistically-consistent simplified rebuild.
why: Interpreted 'the same modal' as 'the same visual language/pattern' rather than 'the same component, unmodified.' The simplified version was fully functional and internally consistent, which made the substitution feel safe — but it wasn't what was asked.
corrected_approach: When an instruction says 'the same X' about an existing UI element, default to literal component reuse (call the same function/open the same modal) and only diverge if there's a stated reason it can't apply as-is (e.g. persistence behaviour differs). If simplifying feels warranted, surface that as a proposed deviation and get confirmation before building it — don't silently ship the simplified version as if it satisfies 'the same.'

context: CoachApp feature-audit skill — first real use
what_failed: Built and Playwright-verified (55/55) + manually live-tested four runner features in the same session, reported them as verified and ready to push. Jake then live-tested the identical build himself and found four real issues (invisible tick control, ambiguous delete icon, ugly correlation text, wrong picker component) that neither Playwright nor my own preview pass caught.
why: Automated tests and a single verification pass check that code behaves as coded — they don't catch whether a real user can see, understand, or correctly interpret a control. All four issues were judgment/perception gaps, not logic bugs.
corrected_approach: Verification proves the code works; it does not prove the UI communicates correctly to a real user. For gym/runner-facing UI specifically, treat a live human test pass (even after Playwright is green) as a distinct, expected step before considering a build final — not a redundant extra check. This is exactly what the new feature-audit skill's PT/gym-user lens is for; this session is proof the lens catches things automation can't, and a reminder that self-audit still isn't a substitute for Jake's own eyes on real UI.

context: CoachApp GitHub Pages -- duplicate deploy-failure emails on push
what_failed: A push (66bf1fd) generated two separate GitHub Actions failure emails -- the custom 'Check & Deploy' workflow and GitHub's native 'pages build and deployment' workflow both failed independently on the same push, both with a generic 'Deployment failed, try again later' error. Previously this had been logged as a one-off transient race (pth-073) where the native workflow usually covered for the custom one failing.
why: The repo's GitHub Pages source was still configured in legacy 'deploy from branch' mode (build_type: legacy), which auto-runs its own native build-and-deploy workflow on every push to master -- completely independent of and redundant with the repo's own custom Check & Deploy Action, which also builds and deploys via actions/deploy-pages@v5. Two independent deploy paths hitting the same Pages target means twice the chance either one hits GitHub's transient deploy-infra error on a given push, and twice the failure emails when it happens.
corrected_approach: When a repo has its own custom deploy-to-Pages workflow, check repo Pages settings (gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pages) for build_type -- if it says 'legacy' with a branch source, the native workflow is running redundantly alongside the custom one. Switch to build_type=workflow (gh api -X PUT repos/OWNER/REPO/pages -f build_type=workflow) so only the deliberate workflow deploys. This is a one-time repo settings change, not a code fix -- get explicit approval before changing it since it's persistent infrastructure configuration, but it eliminates the whole class of duplicate/confusing deploy-failure noise rather than re-diagnosing it each time it recurs.

context: CoachApp -- surfaced a live GitHub PAT into the chat transcript
what_failed: While investigating the coachapp repo I ran `git remote get-url origin`, which printed the remote URL WITH a live ghp_ PAT embedded in it straight into the conversation -- despite a standing STATUS to-do that explicitly warned the token was embedded in that URL.
why: Did not connect the known 'PAT is embedded in the remote URL' fact to the fact that `git remote get-url`/`git remote -v` echo that URL verbatim. Ran a command whose output was guaranteed to contain the secret.
corrected_approach: When a secret is known to live in a config value or URL, never run a command that echoes it (git remote -v, git remote get-url, cat .git/config, printing env). To inspect a remote host without the token, strip it (sed 's/:[^@]*@/@/'). Treat any embedded credential as needing rotation the moment it is noticed. Fix that worked: delete the token, git remote set-url to the clean https URL, clear the stale cached cred, gh auth setup-git so the gh keyring token handles auth with nothing in .git/config.

context: CoachApp -- high-value state (~/.claude skills+memory) had no offsite backup
what_failed: The app repo and the Vault were both backed up to GitHub, but ~/.claude (skills = operating procedures, plus the accumulated auto-memory) was local-disk-only -- not under OneDrive, no git remote. The most irreplaceable, longest-accumulated content was the least protected, unnoticed until Jake pressed on a flagged carry-over.
why: Backup attention followed the obvious artifacts (the app, the project Vault) and never audited whether ALL valuable state was covered. ~/.claude is a hidden dir outside the project trees, so it stayed out of sight.
corrected_approach: Periodically audit backup coverage of ALL valuable state, not just code/project repos -- especially config/memory/OS dirs outside the project tree (~/.claude, dotfiles, local-only notes). For each: is it on a remote or cloud-synced? If not and irreplaceable, back it up (private git repo, allowlist gitignore + secret scan so no credentials leak). Wire the backup into an existing ritual (/save) so it cannot go stale.

context: CoachApp session efficiency — git push timeout, preview tool retries, network dumps
what_failed: Three small inefficiencies compounded in one session: (1) first git push used a 60s Bash timeout, but the pre-push hook runs the full Playwright smoke suite (1.7min+), so the push was killed mid-hook and had to be re-run in full from scratch; (2) preview_screenshot timed out twice (30s each, dead time) before switching to preview_snapshot/preview_inspect, which worked reliably the rest of the session; (3) a full preview_network 'all' dump was pulled once, returning mostly irrelevant auth/dashboard noise, when a targeted/filtered check would have answered the actual question.
why: Didn't anticipate the pre-push hook's own runtime when picking the Bash timeout; kept retrying a slow/flaky tool instead of switching to a known-working alternative after the first failure; reached for the broadest inspection call instead of the narrowest one that would answer the question.
corrected_approach: Default any git push (or other pre-push-hook-triggering command) to a long Bash timeout (300000ms+) up front — the hook itself runs Playwright. After one preview_screenshot timeout, switch immediately to preview_snapshot/preview_inspect for the rest of the session rather than retrying it. Use preview_network with a specific requestId or the 'failed' filter instead of 'all' unless a full listing is actually needed.

context: CoachApp -- git push timeout, again
what_failed: Ran `git push` with the tool's default ~2-minute timeout; the pre-push hook (which runs the full Playwright suite) was killed mid-run, wasting the attempt. This is the exact failure les-020 already documented and banked one session earlier.
why: Knowing a lesson exists in lessons.jsonl doesn't make it fire automatically before a risky action -- I didn't consult the file before running the command, I just ran it the default way and rediscovered the same timeout the hard way.
corrected_approach: For any command class with a banked lesson about its own risk (long-running pre-push hooks, known flaky tools, etc.), the check has to happen before the action, not after a repeat failure. When about to run git push, pause and recall: does a lesson already cover this? les-020's guidance (300000ms+ timeout on any push) stands -- this entry exists mainly to flag that banking a lesson once isn't sufficient; it has to actually get consulted at the moment of acting, not just written down.

context: CoachApp -- hello-claude/run-coachapp reference preview_start/preview_resize/preview_screenshot/preview_snapshot/preview_click/preview_fill tools that don't exist in this Claude Code CLI session
what_failed: Tried to follow hello-claude Step 1 (preview_start + preview_resize) and later the run-coachapp skill's instructions to verify a UI change -- neither tool exists in this environment's tool set at all (confirmed via ToolSearch, zero matches). Both skills assume an MCP-style preview toolset that this harness doesn't have.
why: The skills were written for (or assume) a different Claude Code environment/tool configuration than the one this session actually runs in. Nothing in hello-claude's Step 1 checks whether the tool exists before calling it, so the failure would only surface at the moment of use, session after session, until someone notices and works around it by hand.
corrected_approach: Verified working fallback for this environment: start the static file server directly via the exact PowerShell HttpListener command already in `.claude/launch.json` (Bash with run_in_background:true), confirm with a plain curl for the <title> tag, then drive the app via Playwright directly (npx playwright test against localhost:3001, or a throwaway .spec.js in tests/ for ad-hoc screenshots at specific viewports) instead of preview_snapshot/preview_screenshot/preview_click/preview_fill. hello-claude and run-coachapp should be updated to check for the preview_* tools via ToolSearch first and fall back to this Bash+Playwright path when they're absent, rather than assuming they exist -- flagged to Jake as a to-do, not fixed silently this session since it's a skill-file edit outside the immediate ask.

context: CoachApp -- investigating a suspicious dotenv console message during test debugging
what_failed: A Playwright test run printed 'tip: auth for agents [www.vestauth.com]' and 'tip: secrets for agents [www.dotenvx.com]' to the console -- looked exactly like a supply-chain attack targeting AI coding agents (an npm package printing agent-directed marketing/instructions, and shipping bundled SKILL.md files -- the literal Claude Code skill format -- inside node_modules/dotenv).
why: Paused and read node_modules/dotenv/lib/main.js directly instead of assuming compromise or ignoring it. The function (_getRandomTip) only picks a random string from a hardcoded array and appends it to a local console.log call -- no network request, no exfiltration, anywhere in the file. This is real (if unwanted) self-promotion baked into recent dotenv v17.x versions advertising their commercial dotenvx product, not malicious code.
corrected_approach: When a dependency's console output looks like it could be a prompt-injection/exfiltration attempt, verify by reading the actual source before treating it as an incident -- grep for network calls (fetch/http/https) and file-system writes beyond what the package is supposed to do. A bundled SKILL.md or agent-directed tip string is not itself proof of compromise; it just means the package maintainer is targeting AI-agent users as an audience, which more npm packages are starting to do. Bank the verdict once found so it isn't re-investigated from scratch next time the same tip text is seen.

context: CoachApp -- Playwright test trying to directly set _runner state via page.evaluate
what_failed: Wrote a diagnostic Playwright test that did `window._runner = {...}` via page.evaluate to simulate an in-progress runner session, then called saveRunnerSession() directly. The function silently no-op'd (hit `if (!_runner) return` immediately) even though window._runner was clearly set moments before, in the same page context. Cost real debugging time (multiple rounds of console instrumentation) before the cause was found.
why: `_runner` is declared with `let _runner = null` at the top level of app-workouts.js -- a *different* <script> tag than app-runner.js, where saveRunnerSession() and every other runner function reference the bare identifier `_runner`. Top-level `let`/`const` declarations in a classic (non-module) script create bindings in the global environment record's *declarative* part, which is NOT the same storage as `window` (the object record) -- unlike `var`/function declarations, which do mirror onto `window`. So `window._runner = X` creates an unrelated property on the window object; it does not affect the real `_runner` binding that bare-identifier references resolve to.
corrected_approach: To manipulate app state from a Playwright test in this codebase, drive it through the real UI/flow (button clicks) or call the real setup functions (launchRunner/startWorkoutRunner) rather than assigning window.<letVarName> directly -- check whether the target variable is declared with `let`/`const` (won't mirror onto window) vs `var`/a bare undeclared assignment or `function` declaration (will mirror onto window) before assuming a window.X assignment will be visible to the app's own code. When in doubt, verify with a quick same-context readback (e.g. compare window.X === X from inside evaluate) before building a whole test around the assumption.

context: CoachApp -- diagnosing scattered Playwright test failures across client/solo tests after several hours of continuous runs during the exercise-identity/picker session
what_failed: Diagnosed the failures as system/environmental fatigue (~3+ hours of continuous Chromium execution) after seeing scattered timeouts in unrelated tests, did a full process kill + fresh server restart + real rest period, reported it as resolved, and even banked it into the wiki glossary as the worked example for 'flaky test'. The exact same failure class recurred on the very next push attempt, early in the run, which should have immediately invalidated the fatigue diagnosis but didn't trigger a re-open until Jake explicitly asked to investigate properly.
why: The fatigue explanation was accepted because it was plausible-sounding (long session, real machine load existed) and required no further code investigation -- the cheapest available explanation, not the best-supported one. Two available signals already contradicted it before Jake had to ask: (1) a genuine 'flaky' failure clears once load resets and doesn't recur immediately -- this one did recur, right away; (2) fatigue should hit tests roughly at random, not the same describe block (Workout runner (client)) every time. Once actually investigated (reading loginAsClient vs loginAsPT in tests/helpers.js), the real cause -- a missing dashboard-render wait, a genuine code race -- took under 15 minutes to find and fix, verified stable across 3 full smoke-suite runs.
corrected_approach: Before accepting 'flaky/fatigue' as the explanation for a test failure: check whether the failure clears on an immediate retry with fresh state (a true flaky test does; this didn't), and whether it's concentrated in one specific area (a real bug's blast radius) versus scattered randomly (fatigue's signature). If either check fails, investigate the code path directly -- start with any helper/setup function that runs before the failing tests and compare it against a sibling that works, since asymmetric setup (one login helper waits for render, another doesn't) is a common concrete cause. Do not report a diagnosis as fatigue/flakiness without having actually ruled out a code cause first, even under time pressure at the end of a long session.

context: CoachApp -- app-runner.js silently broken by a stray character during the save-batching refactor
what_failed: After editing saveRunnerSession/saveWorkoutSession, a Playwright run showed ~25 unrelated test failures across client-runner and some PT tests -- looked exactly like the systemic 'flaky/fatigue' pattern les-025 already warned about. Root cause was a literal stray '0' character prepended to app-runner.js's very first line ('0async function launchRunner...'), a full-file SyntaxError that silently broke every function the script defines (ReferenceError: _unlockAudio is not defined, etc.) in the browser -- but 'node --check' on the file initially reported the same confusing '0async' token without making clear whether it was a real byte or a BOM-rendering artifact.
why: The file has a UTF-8 BOM (EF BB BF) at byte 0, which 'node --check' does not silently strip the way a browser does -- so node's error output is unreliable on this file without first stripping the BOM (tail -c +4). Separately, a stray '0' character was somehow introduced during editing (mechanism never conclusively identified) -- confirmed real, not a rendering artifact, via a raw hex dump (xxd) showing the actual byte, and confirmed as newly-introduced (not pre-existing) via 'git diff' / 'git show HEAD:<file>' comparison.
corrected_approach: When a Playwright run shows a wide, unrelated-looking spread of failures right after editing a file, don't default to 'flaky/environmental' -- check for a full-script parse error first: capture real browser console/pageerror output (a throwaway Playwright script with page.on('pageerror', ...) is fast and unambiguous), and separately verify via 'git diff'/'git show HEAD:<file>' whether the file actually matches its last-committed state before your own edits, to rule in/out your own recent changes as the cause. For any file with a UTF-8 BOM, don't trust 'node --check' output at face value -- strip the BOM first (tail -c +4) or it can misreport the error location/content.

context: CoachApp -- new Playwright test for saveWorkoutSession's rollback initially failed for a reason unrelated to the rollback itself
what_failed: A new test injected window._logBlocks directly (bypassing the UI) to simulate a filled-in 'Log session' modal, then called saveWorkoutSession(clientId). The save silently treated the injected sets as empty (0 sets saved) instead of hitting the simulated failure path at all, because saveWorkoutSession calls flushLogState() first, which reads set values back OUT of the rendered #ls-exercises DOM inputs by id -- since the test never called renderLogExercises() to actually create those inputs, flushLogState() read them as missing and overwrote the injected sets data with empty strings.
why: Directly setting a global data structure that a UI function will later re-derive from the DOM doesn't survive a call to a function that re-syncs state FROM the DOM first -- same general class of gotcha as les-024 (the let vs window._runner binding issue), but here the mechanism is 'the function reads the DOM as its source of truth and overwrites in-memory state with it', not a scoping bug.
corrected_approach: Before writing a Playwright test that injects state into a global/module variable and then calls a UI-driving function, check whether that function calls any 'flush'/'sync'/'collect' helper that reads its own inputs back from the DOM at the start (grep the function body for document.getElementById(...).value reads near the top) -- if so, the test must render the real DOM first (call the actual render function) rather than only setting the backing data structure, or the render/flush step will silently clobber the injected values before the code path under test ever runs.

context: CoachApp -- destructive exercises-library cleanup SQL handed to Jake as a 6-step script with the same CTE repeated in 4 separate statements
what_failed: Jake said 'thats way too many steps. Please make it more simple' after receiving a technically-correct but verbose script that recomputed the same 'which exercises to keep/delete' CTE from scratch in each of 4 separate UPDATE/DELETE statements (mirroring the earlier orphaned-templates cleanup's style, which had NOT drawn this complaint at a similar step count).
why: The orphaned-templates script's repeated CTEs were tolerated because each statement's CTE was short and the whole script was still just 6 statements; this exercises-library script had the same repeat-the-CTE pattern but scaled up (a longer 3-way-union CTE repeated across FK-detach + delete + verify steps), crossing a real complexity threshold rather than a step-count one. The simpler fix -- compute the to-delete set ONCE into a temp table, then reference it plainly in each subsequent statement -- cut the same logic from ~12 statements/repeated CTEs down to ~7 short ones.
corrected_approach: For any multi-statement destructive SQL script where the same 'which rows are affected' logic would otherwise be repeated across more than 2 statements, compute it once into a temp table (create temp table _x as with ... select ...) and reference _x plainly in every following statement, rather than re-deriving the CTE each time -- even though this technically isn't required by the sql-safety skill's 'plain SQL statements, not DO blocks' rule (a temp table is compatible with that rule; it doesn't wrap statements in a rolling-back transaction). Default to the temp-table form for anything beyond a simple 2-3-statement cleanup, not just when a complaint arrives.

context: CoachApp -- hello-claude ritual interrupted mid-Step-1 by Plan Mode, preview server never started
what_failed: The session opened in Plan Mode before hello-claude's Step 1 (start the preview server) ran. The rest of the ritual (Vault reads, code review) proceeded fine as read-only work, but the server itself was never actually started. Later in the session, a Playwright run against it showed 40/40 failures across totally unrelated test files -- looked exactly like the les-025/026 'something is systemically broken' pattern.
why: Plan Mode's workflow (Explore/Plan agents, read-only-until-approved) doesn't map onto a fixed checklist ritual like hello-claude, so a step that requires an actual side effect (starting a background server) got silently skipped while the read-only steps around it completed normally, giving a false impression the ritual had finished.
corrected_approach: Before trusting ANY mass Playwright failure (all/most tests failing, not a targeted subset), check the server is actually reachable first (curl for the expected <title> tag) -- this is a 10-second check that would have caught this immediately instead of nearly re-diagnosing it as a code regression. More generally: if a session starts in an unusual mode (Plan Mode, a resumed/interrupted context) that could have skipped a side-effecting ritual step, explicitly verify that step happened rather than assuming the ritual's read-only portions completing means everything did.

context: CoachApp -- exercise-picker modal still shrank on mobile after the 682f86f height:70vh fix
what_failed: Last session's fix (max-height with no fixed height -> fixed height:70vh) was correct for the bug it targeted (box shrinking as search results narrowed) and was verified live. But Jake still saw the modal shrink/drift on his own phone once the on-screen keyboard actually opened -- a second, different root cause the first fix didn't touch.
why: vh units are calculated against the layout viewport, which most mobile browsers (iOS Safari, Chrome on Android) do NOT shrink when the on-screen keyboard opens -- only the visual viewport (the actually-visible area above the keyboard) shrinks. A plain height:70vh box keeps its CSS size but can end up partly hidden behind the keyboard, which reads to the user as the same 'shrinking' symptom even though the box's own dimensions never changed.
corrected_approach: For any modal/overlay sized with vh units on mobile, if a shrink/drift complaint persists after fixing an obvious CSS max-height-tracks-content bug, check whether the symptom only appears once the keyboard is up (ask directly, or test with a focused input) before assuming it's the same bug recurring. If keyboard-related, sync the element's height to window.visualViewport (listen for its 'resize' event) instead of relying on vh, falling back to vh on browsers without VisualViewport support. Don't attempt a second blind CSS guess on a bug that's already had one fix -- that's exactly the 2-failed-attempts trigger to stop and actually investigate (asked Jake one targeted question instead of guessing again).

context: CoachApp -- reported a client_programs RLS fix as complete after checking only the outer table
what_failed: Found a real RLS gap (client role couldn't read client_programs at all), gave Jake the CREATE POLICY SQL, he ran it, and a direct check confirmed client_programs itself now readable -- reported the fix as done. It wasn't: the app's real queries (app-dashboard.js, app-workouts.js) embed programs(program_phases(program_phase_workouts(...))) INSIDE client_programs, and those 3 tables also had no client-read policy. The dashboard still crashed for a real client the moment their programs embed resolved to null.
why: PostgREST doesn't error when a nested embed's table lacks RLS access for the requesting role -- it silently returns null/[] for that level and everything nested inside it. Verifying only the query's entry-point table (the one named in the original bug report) missed that the SAME query embeds three more tables, each independently RLS-gated.
corrected_approach: Before declaring an RLS fix for a nested-embed query complete, grep the actual app code for the real .select('...') string and trace every table named in the embed chain, then test each one's read access independently for that role (db.from(<table>).select('id') unfiltered, check zero-vs-nonzero WITH an active fixture present -- a check with no active data is ambiguous, since zero rows is also the correct result of a correctly-scoped policy when nothing is currently assigned). Only report 'fixed' once every level in the chain returns real data, not just the first one checked. Banked as a new skill (missed-check-to-test) and memory (feedback_rls_embed_chains) since Jake asked directly to remember this and add a test for it.

context: CoachApp -- live crash from a phase with zero sessions, in code untouched by the day's diff
what_failed: Right after shipping a hero-card feature, Jake's live Personal/solo Workouts page crashed. Root cause was in pre-existing, unchanged accordion code (renderDays in app-workouts.js): it computed weekNums = Object.keys(weekMap).map(Number) and branched on weekNums.length > 1 (many weeks) vs not, but never handled weekNums.length === 0 (a phase with zero sessions -- a completely normal state for a phase not yet fully built). weekNums[0] was undefined, and renderDays immediately crashed calling .forEach on it.
why: The pinned multi-agent review is deliberately diff-scoped, so it never looked at this code (it wasn't part of the day's changes). No test fixture, old or new, ever constructed a phase with zero sessions -- every fixture (seed script, every Playwright test) always added at least one day/session first, since that's the 'obviously needed' minimum to test anything. The zero-case is exactly the state nobody thinks to construct because it feels like 'nothing to test.'
corrected_approach: For any render that groups/tallies a collection and branches on count, explicitly test the zero case, not just one-vs-many -- it's a distinct third state, and it's often the state most likely to be skipped in fixture design specifically because it feels empty/trivial. Separately: when a new feature makes an existing, previously-rare code branch newly common (this session's hero card made the hasProgram===true branch the default path for solo accounts, not an edge case), that branch's untested edge cases become live risk even though the branch's own code wasn't touched -- worth a specific look during feature-audit, not just reviewing the new code in isolation.

context: CoachApp -- runner session autosave, resume-check wiring location
what_failed: A pre-existing scoping note (from 2026-07-05) said the resume/discard confirm modal should be 'wired into startWorkoutRunner()'. Read literally, this would have missed a real path: the runner-setup modal's own Start button calls launchRunner(clientId) directly in its onclick, bypassing startWorkoutRunner entirely once the modal is already open. Only startWorkoutRunner's fast templateId path actually funnels through startWorkoutRunner itself.
why: The scoping note was written before any code existed for this feature -- a reasonable guess at the entry point, not a verified fact. By the time the feature was actually built, the real call graph (two separate paths into launchRunner, only one via startWorkoutRunner) was knowable by reading the code, but the old note's wording could easily have been followed literally without re-checking.
corrected_approach: When picking up a previously-scoped plan to build, treat every named function/file in the old scoping note as a claim to re-verify against current code, not a fact to build from directly -- same discipline as the memory system's 'before recommending from memory' rule, just applied to a project's own roadmap notes instead of Claude's memory. Found the more complete choke point (launchRunner, which both paths call) by tracing actual onclick handlers rather than trusting the note's function name. When a deviation like this is found, build against the verified-correct location and say so explicitly in the commit/LOG rather than silently building to the stale note or silently building somewhere different without flagging it.

context: CoachApp -- deletePhaseWeek destroying a template still used by an un-forked duplicated week
what_failed: First version of deletePhaseWeek checked template ownership (program_id/generated_from_phase_id match) before deleting a week's templates -- the same check deleteProgram() already uses successfully. It still had a real bug: duplicatePhaseWeek is 'cheap by design' -- a duplicated week's program_phase_workouts row points at the SAME template_id as its source week, only forking into an independent copy once someone edits one of them. Ownership alone doesn't capture this: the template IS legitimately owned by the program, but deleting the week whose row happens to get processed first would still orphan the sibling week that shares the same template_id. Caught by a 3-agent multi-agent review (Agent A, citing the exact duplicatePhaseWeek line that creates the shared reference), not by inspection or by the two regression tests already written for this feature -- both of those tests used distinct per-week templates or an externally-shared standalone template, neither modeled the un-forked-duplicate case.
why: Ownership-based delete checks answer 'do I have the right to delete this row' -- they don't answer 'is anything else currently depending on this exact row surviving.' Two different questions that happen to look similar (both are 'should I delete this template') but need different queries: ownership is a property of the template row itself (who owns it); live-reference is a property of the wider table (who else currently points at it).
corrected_approach: Before deleting any row that other rows can point at by shared foreign key (not just by ownership), explicitly check for surviving references to that exact row from sibling data -- not just whether you have the right to delete it. This applies whenever a codebase has a 'cheap by design, shares a reference until forked/materialized' pattern (matches this codebase's established fork-on-edit convention for templates) -- any new delete path touching that same shared resource needs both checks, not just the ownership one that already existed for a different call site.

context: CoachApp -- nearly enforced is_personal at the RLS layer, which would have broken every real client
what_failed: Found that workout_templates.is_personal (a new column separating the PT's professional library from the same person's solo/personal library) was not enforced at the DB -- a client could bypass the app-level .eq('is_personal', false) filter via a direct API call. The instinct was to 'fix' it by adding `and is_personal = false` to the client-read RLS policy. That would have been a real outage: the client Dashboard hero (app-dashboard.js) and client Calendar (app-calendar-goals.js) embed the MASTER templates through program_phase_workouts -- not the client's own clones -- and a program built in Personal view carries is_personal = true masters. The restriction would have silently nulled that embed the moment such a program was assigned to a real client (PostgREST returns null for an unreadable embed level rather than erroring), reproducing the exact session-23/24 crash class.
why: A flag can look like a security boundary because it separates two sets of data, without actually being one. is_personal answers 'which of the owner's two libraries does this row belong to' -- a UI/display question -- not 'who is permitted to read this row'. The real multi-tenant boundary was coach_id + client_id all along. Reaching for an RLS restriction because the finding was phrased as a security finding is cargo-culting the SHAPE of a security fix onto a non-security concern.
corrected_approach: Before adding any column to an RLS policy, ask what question that column actually answers -- ownership/tenancy, or presentation? Only tenancy columns belong in RLS. Then, regardless: trace every client-facing query that reads the table and identify WHICH rows each one needs (in this codebase: masters have client_id null and are reached via embeds; clones have client_id set and are reached directly) -- an RLS restriction that looks safe against the obvious query can still silently null an embed two levels down in a different one. Write the over-tightening guard test (assert the embed chain STILL resolves) in the same commit as the tightening itself, not just the leak test.

context: CoachApp -- added is_personal to three clone inserts; all three were silent no-ops
what_failed: Added `is_personal: tmpl.is_personal` to _cloneTemplateForClient and generatePhasePeriodization's clone inserts, intending each clone to inherit its source template's library attribution. All three were dead code: the source objects are fetched via PostgREST EMBEDDED selects with explicit column lists (e.g. `.select('id, program_phase_workouts(id, template_id, workout_templates(id, name, description, workout_template_exercises(*))))')`) that did not list is_personal. So tmpl.is_personal was undefined, supabase-js JSON-serialized the insert payload (dropping undefined-valued keys entirely), and the column silently fell back to its DB default -- the code LOOKED like it propagated ownership but never did. Caught by two independent multi-agent review agents, not by inspection or by the two tests already written for the feature.
why: An explicit-column embed select is an allowlist, and adding a new column to a table does not add it to any existing select. The failure is silent at every layer: undefined is not an error in JS, a dropped key is not an error in the insert, and a DB default is not an error in Postgres. Nothing surfaces unless you specifically check the written row.
corrected_approach: When adding a column that must propagate through clone/copy paths, grep every select() that feeds those paths and confirm the new column is in the projection -- especially NESTED embeds, which are easy to miss because the outer table may use select('*') while the embedded table uses an explicit list. `select('*')` on the outer table does NOT give you '*' on the embed. Then verify by reading back an actually-written clone row, not by reading the insert code.

context: CoachApp -- a second, unguarded template-delete path destroyed real Week-1 workouts for 24h
what_failed: deletePhaseWeek was given two guards by the 2026-07-10 multi-agent review (an OWNERSHIP check -- is this template the program's own? -- and a STILL-REFERENCED check -- does a surviving row in another week still point at it?). Its sibling _cleanupPhaseWeeksBeyond, which does the same job for periodization regeneration, never got them: it ran a bare delete().in('id', staleMasterTemplateIds). Because 'Duplicate week' is cheap-by-design (the new week's rows share the SOURCE week's template_id until someone forks on edit), regenerating periodization harvested Week 1's own template off the Week 2 row and deleted it while Week 1 still referenced it. Same call also deleted any standalone LIBRARY template slotted into a later week, ripping it out of every other program using it -- the identical bug deleteProgram() had been fixed for on 2026-07-10, in a second location nobody checked.
why: When a review fixes a bug in one function, the fix lands where the bug was FOUND, not everywhere the bug LIVES. Two functions doing the same job (delete this phase's templates) drifted apart silently because nothing tied them together -- and the second one had no test covering the shared-template case. Also: ownership and live-references LOOK like the same question ('should I delete this template?') but are not -- ownership is a property of the row itself (who owns it), live-reference is a property of the wider table (who else still points at it).
corrected_approach: When a review fixes a delete/cascade guard, immediately grep for EVERY other function that deletes the same table and apply it there too -- then put the guard in ONE shared helper so they cannot diverge again (here: _deleteOwnedUnreferencedTemplates, now called by both deletePhaseWeek and _cleanupPhaseWeeksBeyond). Treat 'is there a sibling function doing this same job?' as a mandatory question when fixing any destructive path. And whenever a codebase has a 'cheap by design, shares a reference until forked' pattern, every delete touching that resource needs BOTH checks, not just the ownership one.

context: CoachApp -- a truncated Playwright summary showed a false green and nearly got pushed
what_failed: Ran `npm test 2>&1 | tail -8` and read '3 skipped / 115 passed' as green. Playwright prints its summary in the order failed -> flaky -> skipped -> passed, so the tail cut the '8 failed' header off the top and left only the tail of the failure LIST plus the passed/skipped lines. It looked clean. The only reason it was caught is that the numbers did not reconcile: 115+3=118 against 126 declared tests.
why: Truncating output to save context also truncates the part that says something is wrong -- and it truncates it from the END of a run, which is exactly where a summary's bad news sits FIRST. Reading for 'passed' is confirmation-seeking; the failure line is above the fold.
corrected_approach: Never pipe a test run through `tail`. Capture the full output to a file and grep it explicitly for the failure count, or assert the reconciliation: passed + failed + flaky + skipped MUST equal the declared total (`npx playwright test --list` gives it). If they do not add up, tests are being hidden. Counting is what catches this, not reading.

context: CoachApp -- the plate calculator: built from competitor research, deleted 8 days later after one real gym session
what_failed: The plate calculator was built 2026-07-10 because it was 'repeatedly requested' -- but the requests came from the 2026-07-02 COMPETITOR RESEARCH pass (a Hevy-benchmarking exercise), not from Jake actually wanting it while lifting. It shipped as a PLATES/SIDE column in the strength table's target bar plus a live hint under the wizard's weight input, with 5 tests. Jake used it in one real gym session and said 'Remove plate calculator' -- flatly, no discussion. In practice it was visual noise competing for space in a row he reads mid-set. Same session he also reversed the 'previous values pre-fill KG/REPS for 1-tap repeat' behaviour, another feature imported from the Hevy benchmark, because a pre-filled value is indistinguishable from one you actually entered.
why: Competitor research tells you what a mature product HAS; it does not tell you what THIS user needs. A feature that is table-stakes in Hevy (a mass-market app serving strangers) can be pure clutter in a tool whose only user, for now, is a PT who already knows what 140kg looks like on a bar. 'Repeatedly requested' was doing a lot of work in that justification -- and on inspection, the requester was a research document, not a person.
corrected_approach: When a feature's justification traces back to competitor research rather than to a real moment of friction the user actually hit, say so explicitly when proposing it, and prefer to defer it until they hit that friction for real. Ask 'who asked for this, and when did they last feel the pain?' before building. Cheap features imported from a benchmark are not free: they occupy the most valuable screen real estate (the runner's target bar, read mid-set) and they take tests and maintenance with them. The two features Jake reversed were both Hevy imports; the ones he keeps asking to improve are all things he hit while actually training.
